event: Stone Age hunter-gatherers inhabit the region of present-day Equatorial Guinea, leaving behind early tools on the mainland.
Pygmy peoples are among the earliest known inhabitants of the equatorial rainforests of the Río Muni mainland.
The Bantu migration brings agricultural, iron-working peoples into the mainland region, gradually displacing or absorbing earlier inhabitants.
Bantu-speaking Fang and other coastal peoples become established across the Río Muni mainland.
Portuguese navigator Fernão do Pó sights the island later named after him (modern Bioko), claiming it for Portugal.
Portuguese explorers reach the small island of Annobón, reportedly first seen on New Year's Day.
The Treaty of Tordesillas places the Gulf of Guinea islands within the Portuguese sphere of influence.
Portugal attempts to settle Fernando Pó (Bioko) but is driven off by the indigenous Bubi population and disease.
The Dutch West India Company briefly occupies Fernando Pó to use it as a base for the Atlantic slave trade.
The Dutch establish a slave-trading station on the island before withdrawing.
Under the Treaty of El Pardo, Portugal cedes Fernando Pó, Annobón, and trading rights on the mainland coast to Spain.
An early Spanish settlement expedition under Conde de Argelejos collapses due to disease and unrest, and Spain abandons the islands.
Britain leases bases on Fernando Pó from Spain, establishing the settlement of Port Clarence (later Malabo) as an anti-slavery patrol station.
Spain reasserts direct control over Fernando Pó, renaming Port Clarence as Santa Isabel.
Spain appoints its first governor and begins systematic colonial administration of its Gulf of Guinea possessions.
Following the Berlin Conference and the Scramble for Africa, Spain presses claims to the Río Muni mainland.
The Treaty of Paris fixes the borders of Spanish Guinea, granting Spain the Río Muni mainland alongside the islands.
Spain unites its mainland and island territories administratively under the colony of Spanish Guinea.
The Spanish Civil War reaches the colony, where Nationalist forces take control of the territory.
Spain reorganizes the colony into two overseas provinces, Fernando Pó and Río Muni, granting inhabitants Spanish citizenship.
Spain grants the territory limited internal autonomy under the name Equatorial Guinea after a referendum.
Equatorial Guinea gains independence from Spain on 12 October, with Francisco Macías Nguema elected its first president.
Equatorial Guinea is admitted as a member state of the United Nations on 12 November.
A crisis with Spain prompts mass departure of Spanish settlers as Macías consolidates dictatorial power.
Macías bans opposition parties and establishes a one-party state under the United National Party (PUNT).
Macías Nguema declares himself president for life, intensifying a brutal and repressive regime.
Thousands of Nigerian contract laborers are evacuated amid worsening conditions, collapsing the cocoa-based economy.
Lieutenant Colonel Teodoro Obiang Nguema Mbasogo overthrows his uncle Macías in a coup; Macías is tried and executed.
A new constitution is approved by referendum, confirming Obiang as president for a seven-year term.
Equatorial Guinea joins the franc zone economic bloc, later integrating with the CFA franc currency.
The country adopts the Central African CFA franc, replacing the ekwele and aligning with regional economies.
A new constitution nominally introduces multiparty politics, though Obiang's rule remains entrenched.
The first multiparty legislative elections are held but are boycotted by the opposition and criticized as flawed.
Substantial offshore oil reserves are discovered, transforming Equatorial Guinea's economic prospects.
Commercial oil production begins, ushering in an era of rapid, oil-driven economic growth.
Obiang wins a presidential election widely condemned by observers as neither free nor fair.
Obiang is re-elected with a near-unanimous official vote amid opposition boycotts and fraud allegations.
Authorities foil an alleged coup attempt, arresting a group of foreign mercenaries in the 'Wonga Coup' plot.
Obiang wins another presidential term as the country becomes one of sub-Saharan Africa's largest oil exporters.
A constitutional referendum introduces a vice presidency and term limits, consolidating dynastic succession plans.
Equatorial Guinea co-hosts the Africa Cup of Nations football tournament with Gabon.
Equatorial Guinea hosts the Africa Cup of Nations as a late replacement, reaching the semifinals.
Equatorial Guinea adopts Portuguese as a third official language alongside Spanish and French.
Obiang is re-elected to another term, extending one of the world's longest-serving heads of state.
Vice President Teodoro 'Teodorín' Nguema Obiang is convicted in absentia in France for embezzlement and corruption.
The government announces a foiled coup attempt and arrests amid heightened security crackdowns.
Massive explosions at a military barracks in Bata kill scores of people and devastate parts of the city.
Obiang wins re-election with over 94 percent of the vote, securing a sixth term amid opposition criticism.
Declining oil output strains the economy, prompting efforts to diversify and develop the new capital of Ciudad de la Paz (Oyala).
